jokes.text: No, it's not, Bret. Actually, Mel was busy. She actually requested that she can't come today. But if she was here, yes, we'd have two. But unfortunately we're down to one today.
jokes.setup: Band's manager reporting on their audience size
jokes.punchline: Their one regular fan specifically requested not to come
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Murray's optimistic spin on terrible news. The phrasing 'requested that she can't come' is beautifully awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, it's not, Bret. Actually, Mel was busy. She actually requested that she can't come today. But if she was here, yes, we'd have two. But unfortunately we're down to one today.

jokes.text: It's a big guy though, Jemaine. He's taking up three seats. He's got shopping bags with him.
jokes.setup: Murray trying to make one audience member sound better
jokes.punchline: The optimistic spin gets more ridiculous - counting shopping bags as audience
jokes.explanation: Murray's desperate optimism about their pathetic audience situation builds beautifully.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's a big guy though, Jemaine. He's taking up three seats. He's got shopping bags with him.

jokes.text: What, so the majority of the audience is shopping bags? Yeah, most of the audience are shopping bags.
jokes.setup: Jemaine pointing out the absurdity of counting shopping bags
jokes.punchline: Murray earnestly confirming that yes, shopping bags are their main audience
jokes.explanation: Peak Flight of the Conchords - deadpan delivery of completely absurd logic. The matter-of-fact acceptance is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What, so the majority of the audience is shopping bags?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, most of the audience are shopping bags.

jokes.text: Hey, when did he go? When did that guy leave? Yeah, he... he snuck out. Possibly as early as the first song.
jokes.setup: Band finishing their set, looking for their one audience member
jokes.punchline: Their only audience member left during the first song
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of their failure - even their one fan couldn't stick around.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Hey, when did he go? When did that guy leave?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, he... he snuck out. Possibly as early as the first song.

jokes.text: Technically I actually snuck out as well. You snuck out? What do you mean you snuck out?
jokes.setup: Murray admitting their manager also abandoned the show
jokes.punchline: Even their own manager left during their performance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Murray moment - his casual admission of abandoning his own band is beautifully selfish and oblivious.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Technically I actually snuck out as well.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You snuck out? What do you mean you snuck out?

jokes.text: Well, you went shopping. Oh, yeah.
jokes.setup: Jemaine calling out Murray for leaving
jokes.punchline: Murray's casual 'oh yeah' admission about shopping during the gig
jokes.explanation: Murray's complete lack of shame about prioritizing shopping over his band's performance is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, you went shopping.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh, yeah.

jokes.text: That's when I didn't have to pay the meter. Remember the meter was broken when I parked outside your apartment? That's the high point of our career?
jokes.setup: Looking at their career high points graph
jokes.punchline: Their career peak was not paying for parking
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist moment - the pathetic scale of their 'success' is perfectly captured in this mundane parking victory.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's when I didn't have to pay the meter. Remember the meter was broken when I parked outside your apartment? That's the high point of our career?

jokes.text: I still think about it now. It was such a great night.
jokes.setup: Murray reflecting on their career high point
jokes.punchline: He genuinely treasures the memory of free parking
jokes.explanation: Murray's sincere nostalgia for such a trivial moment is perfectly pathetic and endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I still think about it now. It was such a great night.

jokes.text: It's not the '90s. Okay, my point is the difference between cool bands and you is hair.
jokes.setup: Murray making a point about modern music trends
jokes.punchline: His analysis reduces everything to hair
jokes.explanation: Murray's oversimplified analysis of what makes bands cool is characteristically shallow but amusing.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's not the '90s. Okay, my point is the difference between cool bands and you is hair.

jokes.text: I've got hair. True. But most of your hair is body hair, isn't it, Jemaine?
jokes.setup: Jemaine defending that he has hair
jokes.punchline: Murray pointing out it's mostly body hair
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific insult that plays on Jemaine's distinctive appearance.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I've got hair. True.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: But most of your hair is body hair, isn't it, Jemaine?

jokes.text: A moss-green shirt and a tie? Obviously this is for work. Up here is for play, isn't it? No, this is it.
jokes.setup: Jemaine questioning Murray's fashion choices
jokes.punchline: Murray insisting his formal work outfit is also his party look
jokes.explanation: Murray's complete lack of fashion sense and social awareness is perfectly captured.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: A moss-green shirt and a tie? Obviously this is for work. Up here is for play, isn't it?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: No, this is it.

jokes.text: Morning, prime minister. Thank you, m'lord.
jokes.setup: The New Zealand Prime Minister entering
jokes.punchline: Bret's overly formal response with 'm'lord'
jokes.explanation: Bret's awkward formality when meeting authority figures is endearingly inappropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Morning, prime minister.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Thank you, m'lord.

jokes.text: We're gonna have Gary the sheep. He's flying over from New Zealand. Really? Yes, New Zealand's most famous sheep is gonna be there. He's on hiatus.
jokes.setup: Describing attractions for New Zealandtown
jokes.punchline: A sheep being 'on hiatus' like a celebrity
jokes.explanation: Treating a sheep like a celebrity actor with career management is delightfully absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: We're gonna have Gary the sheep. He's flying over from New Zealand. Really? Yes, New Zealand's most famous sheep is gonna be there. He's on hiatus.

jokes.text: New Zealandtown between Chinatown and Little Italy - which isn't exactly accurate geographically
jokes.setup: Describing the location of New Zealandtown
jokes.punchline: Casual admission that the geography makes no sense
jokes.explanation: Deadpan acknowledgment of the absurd geographical placement

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: New Zealandtown between Chinatown and Little Italy - which isn't exactly accurate geographically

jokes.text: We have a chap shearing him. Well, pretending to shear him. Be mime shearing him. Mime shearing him because Gary's got his look. We can't mess with that.
jokes.setup: Explaining how they'll display the sheep
jokes.punchline: Protecting the sheep's 'look' like a celebrity's image
jokes.explanation: The sheep celebrity treatment escalates beautifully with concerns about his 'look' and image.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: We have a chap shearing him. Well, pretending to shear him. Be mime shearing him. Mime shearing him because Gary's got his look. We can't mess with that.

jokes.text: Would you like us to play? Well, no, we're looking for a cool band. They are available. We're considered quite cool, aren't we? In their minds, yes.
jokes.setup: Band offering to play at the opening
jokes.punchline: Prime Minister directly saying they need a cool band, implying they aren't
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment with the polite but devastating rejection of their coolness.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Would you like us to play?

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Well, no, we're looking for a cool band.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: They are available.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: We're considered quite cool, aren't we?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: In their minds, yes.

jokes.text: Ew!
jokes.setup: Jemaine applying hair gel
jokes.punchline: Bret's disgusted reaction to seeing the result
jokes.explanation: Simple reaction beat - functional but not particularly clever or memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Ew!

jokes.text: How's that? That looks cool.
jokes.setup: Jemaine asking for feedback on his gelled hair
jokes.punchline: The visual contrast between his confidence and presumably ridiculous appearance
jokes.explanation: Visual gag relies on audience seeing the gap between confidence and reality.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: How's that?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: That looks cool.

jokes.text: Fashion song sequence
jokes.setup: Musical number about fashion and style
jokes.punchline: Various nonsensical fashion references and stuttered delivery
jokes.explanation: Typical FOTC musical parody with absurd fashion references and their signature awkward delivery.

dialogue.line: Fashion song sequence

jokes.text: You think fashion's your friend, my friend, fashion is danger
jokes.setup: Song building up fashion as important
jokes.punchline: Sudden turn to fashion being dangerous
jokes.explanation: Good lyrical twist that parodies overwrought fashion commentary.

dialogue.line: You think fashion's your friend, my friend, fashion is danger

jokes.text: Posing sequence - posing at the bar, posing sitting down, posing in the distance, posing with my arm, posing with my leg, posing like a swan, posing for a portrait, posing a threat
jokes.setup: List of different ways of posing
jokes.punchline: Escalates to 'posing a threat' - playing on double meaning
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating list that builds to a clever wordplay punchline. Classic FOTC musical comedy structure.

dialogue.line: Posing sequence - posing at the bar, posing sitting down, posing in the distance, posing with my arm, posing with my leg, posing like a swan, posing for a portrait, posing a threat

jokes.text: ¶ posing for a portrait ¶ ¶ posing a threat ¶
jokes.setup: Continuing the posing sequence with expected pose type
jokes.punchline: Shifts from literal posing to idiomatic 'posing a threat'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that shifts the entire meaning while maintaining the pattern - perfect misdirection with linguistic cleverness

jokes.text: That's Bret and Jemaine. You know, my band. They're bloody great.
jokes.setup: Prime Minister impressed by the cool performers
jokes.punchline: Murray taking credit and praising the same band he just called uncool
jokes.explanation: Perfect Murray hypocrisy - quick to take credit when they look good after dismissing them earlier.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: That's Bret and Jemaine. You know, my band. They're bloody great.

jokes.text: They're not from the '70s. They're from New Zealand. Isn't that the same thing? Similar, I suppose.
jokes.setup: Murray commenting on their usual style being from the '70s
jokes.punchline: Equation of New Zealand with being decades behind
jokes.explanation: Sharp self-deprecating humor about New Zealand being behind the times. Economical and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: They're not from the '70s. They're from New Zealand.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Isn't that the same thing?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Similar, I suppose.

jokes.text: Whatever, Murray. Hey, great gig last night. You got a glowing review.
jokes.setup: The boys acting cool and dismissive toward Murray
jokes.punchline: Murray's oblivious enthusiasm about a 'glowing review'
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast between their new cool attitude and Murray's unchanged enthusiasm.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Whatever, Murray. Hey, great gig last night. You got a glowing review.

jokes.text: New Zealand Consulate Newsletter... 'They came, they saw, they conchord. A couple of cool guys took to the stage and wowed the audience with music and up-to-the-minute styles. Move over, refrigerators, here's what's cool.'
jokes.setup: Murray reading a supposedly glowing review
jokes.punchline: Terrible pun on 'conquered' and ridiculous comparison to refrigerators
jokes.explanation: The 'conchord' pun and refrigerator comparison are beautifully terrible - perfectly fitting for a consulate newsletter.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: New Zealand Consulate Newsletter... 'They came, they saw, they conchord. A couple of cool guys took to the stage and wowed the audience with music and up-to-the-minute styles. Move over, refrigerators, here's what's cool.'

jokes.text: Complex chain of command explanation ending with discretionary power over Greg
jokes.setup: Explaining workplace hierarchy
jokes.punchline: Overly complex system for simple tasks
jokes.explanation: Bureaucratic absurdity that goes on too long but captures institutional comedy

dialogue.line: Complex chain of command explanation ending with discretionary power over Greg

jokes.text: Paula grew up next to me. 39 Harcourt Street, Nelson. Mind you, that was all a very very long time ago. Many many years ago now. A lot's happened since then. Things change. People get married to other people.
jokes.setup: Murray introducing Paula to the group
jokes.punchline: His awkward emphasis on time passing and people getting married suggests unrequited feelings
jokes.explanation: Murray's transparent longing and awkwardness around his childhood crush is perfectly uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Paula grew up next to me. 39 Harcourt Street, Nelson. Mind you, that was all a very very long time ago. Many many years ago now. A lot's happened since then. Things change. People get married to other people.

jokes.text: Paula's second in command after me and then you and then Greg. So if I wanted to, I could tell her to do something and then she'd tell you to do it. But I could tell Greg. That's at your discretion.
jokes.setup: Prime Minister explaining the chain of command
jokes.punchline: Overly complex hierarchy for a tiny operation
jokes.explanation: Bureaucratic absurdity played straight - the unnecessary complexity for simple tasks is amusing.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Paula's second in command after me and then you and then Greg. So if I wanted to, I could tell her to do something and then she'd tell you to do it. But I could tell Greg. That's at your discretion.

jokes.text: On a basic level this works, but I think we could use more development.
jokes.setup: Evaluating the chain of command test
jokes.punchline: Treating obvious inefficiency as promising system needing refinement
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery treating absurd bureaucracy as legitimate business practice

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: On a basic level this works, but I think we could use more development.

jokes.text: Paula, could you please ask Murray what time it is? Um, hey, Murray, have you got the time? 2:00. It's 2:00, prime minister. Yes, I heard and I think he heard me.
jokes.setup: Testing the chain of command system
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous inefficiency of the communication chain when everyone can hear each other
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist comedy - the pointless bureaucracy is perfectly demonstrated through this simple time request.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Paula, could you please ask Murray what time it is?

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Um, hey, Murray, have you got the time?

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: It's 2:00, prime minister.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Yes, I heard and I think he heard me.

jokes.text: Actually, you're part Maori, aren't you? No, I'm not doing it. But you are part Maori? Yes, but I'm not doing it. Please? No. Please be the Maori. No.
jokes.setup: Murray trying to get Jemaine to be their Maori warrior
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's firm refusal to be tokenized
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Jemaine's dignity and Murray's cultural insensitivity.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Actually, you're part Maori, aren't you?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, I'm not doing it.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: But you are part Maori?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yes, but I'm not doing it.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Please?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Please be the Maori.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Well, if you don't do it we're gonna have to get Mexicans. You can't get Mexicans to do it. That's culturally insensitive. We've already signed one up so it can't be too sensitive.
jokes.setup: Murray threatening to use inappropriate substitutes
jokes.punchline: His logic that having already hired someone makes it less offensive
jokes.explanation: Murray's backwards logic about cultural sensitivity is perfectly oblivious and wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, if you don't do it we're gonna have to get Mexicans. You can't get Mexicans to do it. That's culturally insensitive. We've already signed one up so it can't be too sensitive.

jokes.text: Hey, where's the gel gone? What happened to all the gel? You know what happened to the gel. You used it. Did you finish the gel on your body hair?
jokes.setup: The boys discovering their gel is gone
jokes.punchline: Accusation about using gel on body hair
jokes.explanation: Solid callback to the body hair joke while showing their dependence on gel for confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Hey, where's the gel gone? What happened to all the gel?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You know what happened to the gel.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You used it.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Did you finish the gel on your body hair?

jokes.text: You've been gelling your beard. Your beard looks good.
jokes.setup: Bret accusing Jemaine of using the gel
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's casual admission and compliment to himself
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's casual vanity about his gelled beard is endearingly self-absorbed.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You've been gelling your beard. Your beard looks good.

jokes.text: Gel throwing fight sequence with 'That was very dangerous' reactions
jokes.setup: Fight over the remaining gel
jokes.punchline: Their overwrought reactions to throwing gel containers
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy with their characteristic overreactions to minor conflicts.

dialogue.line: Gel throwing fight sequence with 'That was very dangerous' reactions

jokes.text: Well, Gary, you must be jet-lagged. Let's get you off to your accommodations. Did you ever think I'd end up being prime minister, Paula? No. Complete surprise to me.
jokes.setup: Prime Minister asking Paula about his success
jokes.punchline: Paula's brutally honest assessment
jokes.explanation: Paula's deadpan honesty about his unexpected rise is perfectly cutting.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: Well, Gary, you must be jet-lagged. Let's get you off to your accommodations.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Did you ever think I'd end up being prime minister, Paula?

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: No. Complete surprise to me.

jokes.text: There's people. They'll see us. No one can see me like this. You go. No, I can't go out there. Not without gel.
jokes.setup: Both afraid to go outside without gel
jokes.punchline: Their complete dependence on hair product for public confidence
jokes.explanation: Their pathetic helplessness without styling product shows how shallow their newfound confidence was.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: There's people. They'll see us.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No one can see me like this.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You go.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: No, I can't go out there. Not without gel.

jokes.text: Oh my God! Oh, it's terrible. What have you done?
jokes.setup: Murray seeing them without gel
jokes.punchline: His horrified reaction to their appearance
jokes.explanation: Murray's over-the-top reaction serves the visual gag of their presumably ridiculous appearance.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh my God! Oh, it's terrible. What have you done?

jokes.text: There's still some gel. There's still a little bit of gel. You're imagining the gel. There's still some. There's no gel left. It's not there.
jokes.setup: Desperately searching for remaining gel
jokes.punchline: Bret's delusional insistence that gel remains
jokes.explanation: Their desperation and Bret's denial about the gel being gone shows their complete dependence.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: There's still some gel. There's still a little bit of gel.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: You're imagining the gel.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: There's still some.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: There's no gel left. It's not there.

jokes.text: What if he's keeping the gel for himself to make himself look good? Don't think so, man. That big ginger head of his looking great.
jokes.setup: Paranoid theory about Murray hoarding gel
jokes.punchline: Bret's sarcastic dismissal based on Murray's appearance
jokes.explanation: Good character moment with Bret's rare sarcastic insight about Murray's perpetually bad hair.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What if he's keeping the gel for himself to make himself look good?

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Don't think so, man. That big ginger head of his looking great.

jokes.text: You can't drink in public over here. - Well, we're in New Zealandtown now. There's nothing they can do about it.
jokes.setup: PM drinking beer in public
jokes.punchline: PM thinks his fake ethnic enclave has sovereignty
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist logic about territorial sovereignty from the PM's delusional perspective

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: You can't drink in public over here.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Well, we're in New Zealandtown now. There's nothing they can do about it.

jokes.text: Quiet, everyone. It's some genuine native Maori warriors. We don't want to disturb them in their natural habitat. If you'd like to take pictures, do it very very quietly.
jokes.setup: Prime Minister giving tour commentary
jokes.punchline: Treating people like animals in a nature documentary
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly uncomfortable commentary that treats humans like zoo animals - perfectly cringey cultural insensitivity.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Quiet, everyone. It's some genuine native Maori warriors. We don't want to disturb them in their natural habitat. If you'd like to take pictures, do it very very quietly.

jokes.text: Okay, everybody off. That was New Zealandtown.
jokes.setup: End of the 'tour' of New Zealandtown
jokes.punchline: The entire experience was apparently just one block/location
jokes.explanation: Perfect anticlimatic end to what was built up as a major cultural attraction - economical and deflating.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Okay, everybody off. That was New Zealandtown.

jokes.text: This isn't my place, is it? This is your place. I must be sleepwalking. Oh no, don't wake me. You could kill me.
jokes.setup: Mel appearing in their apartment unexpectedly
jokes.punchline: Her bizarre sleepwalking explanation and fear of being woken
jokes.explanation: Mel's characteristic bizarre behavior and weird logic perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: This isn't my place, is it? This is your place. I must be sleepwalking. Oh no, don't wake me. You could kill me.

jokes.text: Bird watching. I love to watch birds.
jokes.setup: Searching through her pockets for mousse
jokes.punchline: Random declaration about bird watching
jokes.explanation: Classic Mel non-sequitur - random and perfectly in character but not particularly strong comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Bird watching. I love to watch birds.

jokes.text: This is my sock.
jokes.setup: Looking for mousse in her pockets
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact identification of sock as not-mousse
jokes.explanation: Simple absurdist moment - why she has a sock in her pocket is the real joke.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: This is my sock.

jokes.text: You come in here without any mousse? What's wrong with you? I don't know who you are anymore.
jokes.setup: Mel not having the mousse she thought she might have
jokes.punchline: Bret's disproportionate anger and dramatic reaction
jokes.explanation: Bret's overwrought reaction to the mousse disappointment shows how desperate they've become.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: You come in here without any mousse? What's wrong with you? I don't know who you are anymore.

jokes.text: You come in here without any mousse? What's wrong with you? I don't know who you are anymore.
jokes.setup: Mel doesn't have the hair product they need
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's dramatic overreaction to cosmetic disappointment
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's melodramatic response treats mousse availability as a personal betrayal

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You come in here without any mousse? What's wrong with you? I don't know who you are anymore.

jokes.text: Glue? This should work. Yeah yeah, this is good. It's just like gel. Same viscosity.
jokes.setup: Finding glue as a gel substitute
jokes.punchline: Their willingness to use glue and technical justification
jokes.explanation: Their desperation leads to using glue - the 'same viscosity' line shows their attempt at rationalization.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Glue?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: This should work. Yeah yeah, this is good. It's just like gel. Same viscosity.

jokes.text: Jemaine, I stuck my head to my hand. You idiot, Bret. You've got a pillow on your head. What? Where? Other side. Oh, we're both idiots. Idiot, man.
jokes.setup: The glue has caused problems for both of them
jokes.punchline: Their mutual realization of their stupidity
jokes.explanation: Excellent physical comedy payoff - both suffering consequences of their desperation in different ridiculous ways.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Jemaine, I stuck my head to my hand.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You idiot, Bret. You've got a pillow on your head.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: What? Where? Other side.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Oh, we're both idiots.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Idiot, man.

jokes.text: Hi, Greg, I need some anti-glue. What's anti-glue, Murray? Well, it's anti-glue, isn't it? It gets rid of glue. I don't think that exists, Murray.
jokes.setup: Murray asking Greg for anti-glue
jokes.punchline: Murray's circular logic about what anti-glue is
jokes.explanation: Murray's confident explanation of a nonsensical concept is perfectly in character - his certainty about 'anti-glue' is wonderfully stupid.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Hi, Greg, I need some anti-glue.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: What's anti-glue, Murray?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, it's anti-glue, isn't it? It gets rid of glue.

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: I don't think that exists, Murray.

jokes.text: He's been shorn off. The guy who was supposed to mime shear him sheared him for real. He's basically bald. Yes. So there's your star, timid and nervous.
jokes.setup: Murray explaining what happened to Gary the sheep
jokes.punchline: The mime shearer actually sheared the celebrity sheep
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback that escalates the sheep celebrity concept - the failure of the mime shearing is beautifully absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: He's been shorn off. The guy who was supposed to mime shear him sheared him for real. He's basically bald. Yes. So there's your star, timid and nervous.

jokes.text: And to make matters worse, the guy next to him barbecuing... I think he's barbecuing lamb. Gary can tell. He's livid.
jokes.setup: Things getting worse for Gary the sheep
jokes.punchline: Gary witnessing lamb being barbecued at his own event
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark escalation - the sheep celebrity having to watch his species being barbecued is both absurd and horrifying.

dialogue.line: And to make matters worse, the guy next to him barbecuing... I think he's barbecuing lamb. Gary can tell. He's livid.

jokes.text: What side of the road do you drive on? The middle. What language do you speak there? We pretty much just make it up as we go along. Really? Yeah, that's why the people from there are so hard to understand.
jokes.setup: Tourist asking questions at information booth
jokes.punchline: Completely wrong answers about New Zealand
jokes.explanation: Self-referential joke about New Zealanders being hard to understand while giving absurd misinformation.

dialogue.speaker: Tourist
dialogue.line: What side of the road do you drive on?

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: The middle.

dialogue.speaker: Tourist
dialogue.line: What language do you speak there?

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: We pretty much just make it up as we go along.

dialogue.speaker: Tourist
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Greg
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's why the people from there are so hard to understand.

jokes.text: It wasn't the gel that made you cool. It wasn't? It was the confidence the gel gave you.
jokes.setup: Murray explaining their coolness
jokes.punchline: The distinction between gel and confidence from gel
jokes.explanation: Good insight moment that could be meaningful if not immediately undercut - Murray showing rare wisdom.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It wasn't the gel that made you cool.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It wasn't?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It was the confidence the gel gave you.

jokes.text: Yeah, it was the hair gel, guys. Sorry. We're not cool? No.
jokes.setup: After their performance fails without gel
jokes.punchline: Murray immediately contradicting his earlier wisdom
jokes.explanation: Perfect Murray moment - his immediate reversal of his wise advice when proven wrong is beautifully fickle.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, it was the hair gel, guys. Sorry. We're not cool? No.

jokes.text: Forget it, Bryan. It's New Zealandtown.
jokes.setup: Prime Minister's failed romantic moment
jokes.punchline: Parody of 'Forget it Jake, it's Chinatown' ending
jokes.explanation: Excellent film noir parody ending that ties together the episode's themes with a classic movie reference.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Forget it, Bryan. It's New Zealandtown.
